title: Suhrawardi's Illuminationist (Ishraq) Philosophy
subtitle: The hierarchy of lights, the cosmic North (Na-koja-abad), the mundus imaginalis, and the Guide of light as the core of illuminationist daimonology

summary.one_line: Suhrawardi's Hikmat al-Ishraq as a complete system of luminous ontology: from the Light of Lights (Nur al-Anwar) through the archangelic lights to human souls, with the mundus imaginalis as the int...
summary.scope: Primary: Corbin's Man of Light in Iranian Sufism (chapters on Sohravardi). Secondary: Philosophy of Illumination (Walbridge trans.), Hayakil al-Nur.

sources.label: Henry Corbin — The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism (ch. II-III on Sohravardi)
Suhrawardi — The Philosophy of Illumination (Hikmat al-Ishraq), Walbridge trans.
Suhrawardi — The Book of Radiance (Partow Nameh), Ziai trans.

body.text: Between the world of pure spiritual Lights (Luces victoriales, the world of the 'Mothers' in the terminology of Ishraq) and the sensory universe, at the boundary of the ninth Sphere (the Sphere of Spheres) there opens a mundus imaginalis which is a concrete spiritual world of archetype-Figures, apparitional Forms, Angels of species and of individuals.
body.notes: The mundus imaginalis as the eighth climate (keshvar) of Suhrawardi's cosmology

body.text: The 'eighth' keshvar, the mystical Earth of Hurqalya with emerald cities; it is situated on the summit of the cosmic mountain, which the traditions handed down in Islam call the mountain of Qaf. This is the paradisal Earth of Light, the world of Hurqalya, an Orient intermediate between the 'lesser Orient,' which is the soul's rising to the highest point of its desire and consciousness, and the 'greater Orient,' which is the further spiritual Orient, the pleroma of pure Intelligences.
body.notes: The heavenly Earth of Hurqalya at the summit of Mount Qaf — Na-koja-abad

body.text: The mountain of Qaf is this Sphere of Spheres surrounding the totality of the visible cosmos; an emerald rock, casting its reflection over the whole of the mountain of Qaf, is the keystone of this celestial vault, the pole. This is the threshold onto the 'climate of the Soul,' a world made wholly of a subtle 'matter' of light, intermediate between the world of the Cherubinic pure Lights and the world of physis.
body.notes: The Emerald Rock at the pole as the keystone of the celestial vault

body.text: The central intuition is made explicit in the form of a number of Figures, amongst which the role assumed by the Hermetic figure of the Perfect Nature (al-tiba' al-tamm) is especially noteworthy. An essential detail: the Arabic tradition of Hermetism is the only one that allows us to give this Figure its context. From it we learn that Perfect Nature is the heavenly paredros, the Sage's Guide of light.
body.notes: Perfect Nature as the heavenly paredros in Suhrawardi's Hermetic lineage

body.text: Thou, my lord and prince, my most holy angel, my precious spiritual being, Thou art the Spirit who gave birth to me, and Thou art the Child to whom my spirit gives birth... Thou who art clothed in the most brilliant of divine Lights... may Thou manifest Thyself to me in the most beautiful of epiphanies, show me the light of Thy dazzling face, be for me the mediator... lift the veils of darkness from my heart...
body.notes: Sohravardi's psalm to Perfect Nature — the personal liturgy of the Ishraqi sage

body.text: Hermes kept vigil all night long, meditating in the 'temple of light' (haykal al-nur, his own microcosm), but a sun shone in this night. When the 'pillar of dawn' burst forth... Hermes saw an Earth being swallowed up and with it the 'cities of the oppressors' drowning in the divine wrath. This downfall of the sensory, material world... is heralded by the outburst of light of the 'midnight sun' (as in Apuleius: media nocte vidi solem coruscantem).
body.notes: The midnight sun vision of Hermes in Sohravardi — the central ecstatic moment

body.text: The Fravartis are, in Mazdean cosmogony, feminine entities, heavenly archetypes of all the beings composing the Creation of light. Each being has his fravarti in the heavenly world which assumes the role of his guardian angel. What is more, all the Celestial beings, gods, angels and archangels, even Ohrmazd himself, have their respective fravarti. Syzygies of light, 'light upon light.'
body.notes: The Zoroastrian prototype of the celestial double that Suhrawardi revived

body.text: The Avesta (Yasht 57) has him dwelling in triumph on the summit of the highest of mountains (Haraiti Bareza, the Alborz). This very abode is 'self-illuminated within, and adorned on the outside with stars'... the angel Sraosha watches over the sleeping world; he is the guardian angel and the head of a brotherhood of migrants who 'keep watch' on the world.
body.notes: The angel Sraosha as the Angel of initiation dwelling at the cosmic north

body.text: The Latin equivalent would be the expression cognitio matutina, used in Renaissance Hermetism... Whereas the evening knowledge, 'occidental,' cognitio vespertina, is the outer man's knowledge — knowledge of the outside of things — the morning knowledge, 'oriental,' cognitio matutina, is the knowledge of the man of light, having attained the 'abode which secretes its own light,' that is to say the Emerald Rock, this being the knowledge which is self-consciousness.
body.notes: The morning knowledge as the essence of Suhrawardi's Oriental theosophy

body.text: The exile, the stranger, faces up to the powers of oppression which try to force him to forget and to conform to the demands of their collective mastery... He has to reach the summit, the Emerald Rock that rises up before him like the translucent wall of a mystical Sinai; there, on the threshold of the pleroma of Light, the pilgrim meets his Perfect Nature, his Holy Ghost, in an ecstasy of anticipation corresponding, in the Mazdean dramaturgy, to the meeting in the dawn with the celestial Person, at the entrance to the Chinvat Bridge.
body.notes: The Recital of the Occidental Exile as Suhrawardi's master narrative of the soul's return

gaps: Full hierarchy of lights from Hikmat al-Ishraq
Direct extraction from Suhrawardi's own text
